summary: I was born in Romania in 1967, where I met and married my husband, Ismail Said, who is Egyptian. We lived there with our daughter Aida and even started a family business. Romania was our home until we won the U.S. Diversity Visa lottery, which changed our lives forever.

My husband, Ismail Said, is from Egypt, and although we lived in Romania, his Egyptian heritage was always part of our family story. We raised our daughter Aida together in Romania before deciding to immigrate to the United States. Egypt represents an important part of our family's background and identity.

We entered the United States Diversity Visa lottery mostly as a joke, sending in our application without much hope. To our surprise, about a year later, we received a letter saying we had been selected. This lottery was our gateway to immigrating to the U.S. in 2002, despite the odds of over 11 million applicants that year.

We immigrated to the United States in January 2002 after winning the Diversity Visa lottery. Despite arriving with no friends or family, we quickly adapted by renting an apartment, enrolling our daughter in school, and finding jobs. Over 15 years, we've built a life here with three children and our own home, fulfilling what I consider my American dream.

Our visa interview took place at the United States Embassy on September 4th, 2001, just a week before the September 11 attacks. I still remember the visa officer joking with our five-year-old daughter, Aida, and telling her she had to visit Disneyland. The embassy was the crucial step in our immigration process, where we faced both hope and uncertainty.

During our visa interview at the U.S. Embassy, the officer joked with our daughter Aida and told her she had to visit Disneyland someday. That moment stuck with me as a symbol of the new life and opportunities that awaited us in America. Disneyland represented a hopeful and joyful future for our family.

Our visa interview was just a week before the September 11 attacks in 2001, and the tragedy deeply shocked and pained us. It made us question whether immigrating to the U.S. was the right choice, but after careful thought, we decided to proceed. That event marked a difficult but defining moment in our journey to start a new life in America.

We arrived in the United States through Detroit Metropolitan Airport in January 2002, carrying only six suitcases and no connections in the country. From there, we stayed in a hotel briefly before settling into an apartment and beginning our new life. That airport was the gateway to everything that followed for our family.
